name: Calumet Iron Mine
short_description: Explore the historic Calumet Iron Mine on BLM land near Turret. This high-elevation site offers collectors access to iron minerals and quartz specimens with moderate hiking and alpine terrain.
description:
The Calumet Iron Mine sits at 8,887 feet elevation in the mineral-rich Chaffee County area of central Colorado. This BLM-managed rockhounding site attracts collectors interested in iron oxide minerals, quartz, and feldspar that formed within the region's Tertiary-age metamorphic and igneous host rocks. The site spans moderate terrain with mixed conifer forest and alpine meadow vegetation typical of the Colorado Rockies at this elevation.

Historically, the Calumet area saw iron mining activity when Colorado's mining boom reached the Upper Arkansas River valley. Today, the old mine workings remain visible and add geological and historical interest to the site. Collectors will find scattered iron minerals and quartz specimens both in old tailings and in natural outcrops on surrounding hillsides. The moderate slope terrain and accessible hiking routes make this a viable destination for rockhounds with intermediate experience.

What to expect: Variable specimen quality, with better finds typically near old mine dumps and exposed outcrop areas. The altitude requires acclimatization, and weather changes rapidly at this elevation. Most visitors spend 2 to 4 hours exploring the immediate mine area and surrounding slopes.

This location offers the advantage of BLM public access without permit requirements, making it ideal for recreational collectors planning a Colorado Rockies rockhounding trip. The combination of mining history, high-altitude geology, and scenic mountain terrain makes Calumet a worthwhile stop for anyone collecting in the region.
history: The Calumet Iron Mine area developed during Colorado's late 19th and early 20th century iron mining era, when demand for iron ore and fluxing minerals drove exploration throughout the Upper Arkansas drainage. The mineral deposits formed during Tertiary-age magmatic and hydrothermal activity that affected the Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks, creating iron oxide concentrations that miners extracted and processed for regional smelters.
tips: Search the old mine dumps and tailings piles first, where weathering has exposed fresh iron oxide and quartz specimens. Bring a geology hammer and hand lens to examine feldspar crystals and mica inclusions in the surrounding granitic outcrops. Look for reddish and dark gray iron oxides in the upper layers of old tailings, and check exposed rock faces on the moderate slopes for quartz veins running through the host rock. Early morning light on the east-facing slopes helps spot specimens against the alpine grass.
terrain_notes: Elevation 8,887 feet with moderate slope terrain. Mixed conifer forest with alpine meadow clearings. Unpaved road access leads to parking area near old mine workings. From parking, most productive collecting areas require 0.25 to 1 mile of hiking on established trails and cross-country terrain. Footing is rocky and uneven with loose talus sections common. Summer snowmelt can create wet patches. Altitude exposure requires physical fitness and acclimatization for lowland visitors.
best_season: June - September. High elevation means snow blocks access October through May. Summer afternoons bring afternoon thunderstorms typical of the Colorado high country, so start early and descend by early afternoon.
written_directions: From Turret, take County Road 306 (Chalk Creek Road) northwest approximately 6 miles toward the Sawatch Range. Watch for Forest Service and BLM signs marking the Calumet area. Turn onto Forest Service Road 307 and follow it northeast for approximately 2 miles to the mine area parking pullout at 8,800+ feet elevation. Road surface is rough and unimproved; high-clearance vehicle recommended, especially in early summer.
road_conditions: Unimproved Forest Service road with rough surface, washboard texture, seasonal mud and snow. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Passenger vehicles may struggle with ruts; AWD preferable.
parking_notes: Small unpaved pullout area near old mine workings. Space for 3-4 vehicles. No facilities. Parking is informal on BLM land.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Do not damage or remove specimens from protected outcrops. Respect any posted closure areas near mine entrances for safety. Pack out all trash.
rules:

nearest_services: Turret and Salida approximately 8-12 miles south. Salida has full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. Turret is a small community with limited services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with uneven rocky footing and loose talus common. No wheelchair access. Steep elevation gain in places. High altitude (8,887 feet) requires acclimatization and physical fitness. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or altitude sensitivity.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Calumet Iron Mine near Turret, Colorado?
faq.answer: Collectors find iron oxide minerals, quartz, feldspar, and mica at Calumet Iron Mine. Iron oxides appear as reddish and dark gray specimens, particularly in old mine tailings. Quartz occurs both in specimens and in veins through the granitic host rock. Feldspar and mica are visible in exposed outcrops on the surrounding slopes.

faq.question: Is Calumet Iron Mine open to rockhounding without a permit?
faq.answer: Yes. Calumet Iron Mine sits on public BLM land and requires no permit for recreational collecting. You may collect up to 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best season to visit Calumet Iron Mine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: June through September is the optimal season. Snow blocks access from October through May. Summer thunderstorms typically develop in the afternoons at this 8,887-foot elevation, so plan to collect in early morning and descend by early afternoon.

faq.question: How do I reach Calumet Iron Mine from Turret?
faq.answer: From Turret, take County Road 306 (Chalk Creek Road) northwest approximately 6 miles toward the Sawatch Range. Turn onto Forest Service Road 307 and follow it northeast about 2 miles to the mine area parking pullout. Road surface is rough and unimproved; a high-clearance or AWD vehicle is recommended.

faq.question: Is Calumet Iron Mine suitable for beginner rockhounds or families?
faq.answer: Calumet is better suited for intermediate to advanced rockhounds. The moderate terrain, high elevation (8,887 feet), and need for acclimatization make it challenging for young children. Kids should be at least 12 years old and experienced hikers. Families with younger children may prefer lower-elevation Colorado rockhounding sites.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to rockhounding at Calumet Iron Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, hand lens or magnifying glass, sturdy hiking boots, and a backpack for specimens (25 lb limit per day). Pack plenty of water (altitude increases dehydration risk), sun protection, rain gear for afternoon thunderstorms, and a map. A 4x4 or high-clearance vehicle is essential for accessing the rough Forest Service Road 307.

meta_description: Find iron oxides, quartz, and feldspar at Calumet Iron Mine near Turret, Colorado. BLM public land, no permit needed. June-September.
